title: Demand-Driven Context: A New Framework for Architecture Knowledge
description: How to build architecture knowledge systems that deliver the right context at the right time, driven by actual developer needs rather than top-down documentation mandates.

slides.title: Demand-Driven Context
slides.subtitle: A New Framework for Architecture Knowledge
slides.author: Raj Navakoti
slides.notes: Welcome everyone. Today I want to talk about a fundamental problem in how we manage architecture knowledge in large organizations.

slides.title: The Problem
slides.subtitle: Why architecture documentation fails
slides.notes: Let's start with something we all recognize.

slides.title: Documentation Graveyard
items: Architecture docs are written once, read never
Confluence pages become stale within weeks
ADRs capture decisions but not the reasoning journey
New team members learn from code, not docs
Knowledge lives in people's heads, not systems
slides.notes: Raise your hand if you've experienced at least 3 of these. Every hand should be up.

slides.title: The Core Insight
body: Documentation fails because it's supply-driven. Someone decides what to write, writes it, and hopes someone reads it.
What if we flipped the model? What if architecture knowledge was demand-driven — surfaced exactly when and where a developer needs it?
slides.notes: This is the key mental shift. From push to pull.

slides.title: Supply vs. Demand
right: Deliver at the moment of need
slides.notes: The left column is what we've been doing. The right is where we need to go.

slides.title: Context at the Point of Decision
slides.caption: Architecture context surfaced where developers actually work
slides.notes: Demo: show how context appears in the IDE when a developer opens this file.

slides.title: Implementation Steps
items: 3. Build context delivery pipelines (IDE, PR, CLI)
4. Measure: did the context prevent a mistake?
slides.notes: These are the concrete steps you can take back to your teams on Monday.

slides.title: Results
body: Architecture-related PR comments dropped 40%. Onboarding time for new engineers reduced by 3 weeks. Zero stale documentation — because context is generated from living sources.
slides.notes: These numbers are from our pilot program. Your mileage may vary but the direction is consistent.

slides.title: Q&A
slides.subtitle: raj@navakoti.dev | @rajnavakoti | github.com/rajnavakoti
slides.notes: Open floor for questions. Common ones: How does this work with microservices? How do you handle conflicting context? What tools did you use?
